rationale: Huh et al. is cited in the core theoretical development, not just in a background list: the paper summarizes its selective cross-stimulus sensitization results and uses them to justify hypotheses about complement-based assortments. The citation appears in the Behavioral Effects section and is tied to the argument for why complements shown together should increase unplanned consideration and buying. Although the citing paper introduces additional mechanisms and moderators (especially visualization), the cited study provides a meaningful conceptual base that the new work carries into a new managerial context.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on Huh et al. (2016)'s finding that exposure to one item selectively increases desire for complementary items. It extends that idea to retail assortment design, proposing and testing that complement-based category organization can lift consideration, purchases, and spending in store contexts.
